did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid framework of fully convolutional networks (FCNs) based on VGG-16 and GoogLeNet for skin lesion segmentation, and a deep residual network (DRN) combined with a support vector machine (SVM) for melanoma detection/classification. These AI/ML methods were applied to dermoscopy images from the ISBI 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets to obtain segmentation and classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving a proposed 'hybrid fully convolutional networks-based' framework for skin lesion segmentation and melanoma detection. The paper details specific methods (VGG-16, GoogLeNet, DRN, SVM), datasets used (ISBI 2016, ISIC 2017), and reports original quantitative results (accuracy metrics) in tables and text, which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: Hybrid fully convolutional networks-based skin lesion segmentation and melanoma detection using deep feature
journal: International Journal of Imaging Systems and Technology
brief_summary: This study proposes a two-stage hybrid framework for skin lesion segmentation and melanoma detection to aid in early diagnosis. The segmentation stage utilizes a hybrid fully convolutional network combining VGG-16 and GoogLeNet architectures. For classification, the system extracts deep features using a deep residual network (DRN) and hand-crafted features, which are then processed by a support vector machine. The model was evaluated on the ISBI 2016 and ISIC 2017 dermoscopy image datasets. The proposed method achieved a classification accuracy of 0.8892 on the ISBI 2016 dataset and 0.853 on the ISIC 2017 dataset. These results demonstrate that the hybrid approach outperforms existing methods in both segmentation and classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for detection and classification. In ISBI 2016 dataset, 727 images were melanoma (training) and 52 were melanoma (testing). In ISIC 2017 dataset, 374 images were melanoma.

Used as the negative class for binary classification in ISBI 2016 (173 training, 219 testing). In ISIC 2017, benign images (seborrheic keratosis and benign nevi) were grouped together as the non-melanoma class (1626 images).
Specific benign class identified in the ISIC 2017 dataset. 254 images were seborrheic keratosis. Grouped with benign nevi for the final binary classification task.
Specific benign class identified in the ISIC 2017 dataset. 1372 images were benign nevi. Grouped with seborrheic keratosis for the final binary classification task.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a hybrid fully convolutional network (FCN) based on VGG-16 and GoogLeNet to segment skin lesions from dermoscopy images. The output is a pixel-level segmentation mask (indicated by red contours in results). Evaluation metrics include Dice coefficient and Jaccard index.

The paper performs melanoma detection/classification using a deep residual network (DRN) and support vector machine (SVM) on extracted features from segmented lesions. The task is binary classification (melanoma vs. nonmelanoma/benign). Evaluation metrics include accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on ISBI 2016 and ISIC 2017 datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016 Dataset (Skin Lesion Analysis for Melanoma Detection)
datasets_used.description: A public challenge dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive containing quality-controlled dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized Part 1 for segmentation and Part 3 for classification.

use.training: Used for segmentation training (70% of Part 1 training set, approx. 630 images) and classification training (Part 3 training archive, 629 images total: 508 melanoma, 121 nonmelanoma).
use.testing: Used for segmentation testing (30% of Part 1 training set, approx. 270 images) and classification testing (Part 3 testing archive, 271 images total: 52 melanoma, 219 nonmelanoma).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Dataset
datasets_used.description: The next version of the ISIC challenge dataset providing 2000 skin lesion images with masks for segmentation, super pixel masks, and annotations for classification. It contains three categories: Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Nevus.

use.training: Used as the training data source for the classification model (images from ISBI 2016 were used to train, but ISIC 2017 images were the target for testing; however, the text states 'ISBI 2016 dataset is used as the training data to classify these images' referring to ISIC 2017 testing, implying the model trained on ISBI 2016 was applied to ISIC 2017. The ISIC 2017 dataset itself was primarily used for testing/evaluation in this specific experimental setup, though it is a training dataset for the broader challenge).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification performance. Images were grouped into two classes for binary classification: Melanoma (374 images) and Benign (1626 images, comprising 254 Seborrheic Keratosis and 1372 Benign Nevi).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Dice coefficient, Jaccard index) without statistical significance testing.

segmentation.notes: The proposed work gave +4.7% better accuracy than VGG-16, +3.6% than GoogleNet, and +0.01% than FCRN-50.

classification.notes: The proposed work gave +6.32% better accuracy than VGG-16, +4.22% than GoogleNet, and +3.42% than FCRN-50. The training/testing split for classification was imbalanced (training: melanoma/non-melanoma = 508/121; testing: melanoma/non-melanoma = 52/219).

The model trained on ISBI 2016 was used to classify ISIC 2017 images. The ISIC 2017 dataset contained 374 melanoma images and 1626 benign images (254 seborrheic keratosis and 1372 benign nevi). The proposed work outperformed other works on this dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) for segmentation; Deep Residual Network (DRN) combined with Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-16 and GoogLeNet architectures were used as the basis for the FCNs; these networks were pretrained on ImageNet (implied by reference to ILSVRC14 performance and standard practice for these architectures, though explicit pretraining weights loading is not detailed in the provided text, the text states 'FCN of VGG16 and GoogLeNet are used'). The DRN architecture is similar to FCRN used in previous work.
architecture_used.input_size: Arbitrary size (FCNs take input of arbitrary size and produce correspondingly sized output)

components: FCN based on VGG-16 (specifically FCN-8s architecture with two skip layers)
segmentation_stage.fusion_method: Average of predicted scores from both networks
segmentation_stage.layers: FCN-8s formed by combining layers of feature hierarchy and refining spatial precision; output stride divided into half from a 16 pixel stride layer; 1x1 convolution layer added on top of pool4

classification_stage.drn_details: Similar to FCRN; 7x7 average pooling layer followed by 16th residual block; 50 layers residual network
classification_stage.hand_crafted_features: Local Texton XOR Patterns (LTxXORP) and HSV histograms
classification_stage.feature_fusion: DRN features and LTxXORP features fused to form a new feature vector

training_details.implementation: Python on NVIDIA TITAN X GPU
training_details.optimization: Stochastic gradient descent on loss computed from whole images
training_details.loss_function: Sum over spatial dimensions of the final layer
training_details.dataset_split: 70% training, 30% testing for ISBI 2016; ISBI 2016 used for training, ISIC 2017 used for testing

classification_or_output_head.segmentation_output: Pixel-level segmentation mask (predicted score for each pixel)
classification_or_output_head.classification_output: Binary classification (Melanoma vs. Nonmelanoma) via SVM

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB color images to HSV color space for feature extraction (specifically for Local Texton XOR Patterns and HSV histograms)
Application of a liquid polarization filter to remove surface skin reflectance (mentioned in the context of dermoscopy image acquisition and related work)
Elimination of variable lighting effects (mentioned in the context of related work to improve segmentation efficiency)
Removal of vignetting effect (mentioned in the context of related work to improve segmentation efficiency)
Hair removal from skin lesions (mentioned in the context of related work to improve segmentation efficiency)
conclusions: The proposed two-stage hybrid framework, combining fully convolutional networks (FCNs) based on VGG-16 and GoogLeNet for segmentation and a deep residual network (DRN) with hand-crafted features for classification, improves both segmentation and classification accuracy for melanoma detection.
The hybrid segmentation approach, which averages the predicted scores from FCN-VGG16 and FCN-GoogleNet, yields better results than applying the networks individually.
The proposed method achieved a classification accuracy of 0.8892 on the ISBI 2016 dataset and 0.853 on the ISIC 2017 dataset, outperforming existing works including VGG-16, GoogleNet, and FCRN-50.
Previous works based solely on hand-crafted features demonstrated lower classification accuracy due to interclass similarity between melanoma and nonmelanoma lesions.
Segmentation remains a challenging task due to variations in skin tone, lighting, and lesion characteristics such as color, texture, shape, size, and location.
The study suggests that the proposed architecture could be enhanced in future work by replacing the two pretrained models with a single enhanced model to reduce complexity.
important_remarks: The classification model was trained exclusively on the ISBI 2016 dataset and tested on the ISIC 2017 dataset, representing a cross-dataset evaluation rather than internal validation, which raises concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging protocols.

The training data for classification in ISBI 2016 was highly imbalanced (508 melanoma vs. 121 nonmelanoma), and the testing data was also imbalanced (52 melanoma vs. 219 nonmelanoma), which likely biased the model towards the majority class and inflated specificity while suppressing sensitivity.

The paper reports no statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons, making it impossible to determine if the reported improvements over baseline models are statistically significant.

The proposed method combines deep features from a Deep Residual Network (DRN) with hand-crafted Local Texton XOR Patterns (LTxXORP) and HSV histograms, introducing complexity and potential redundancy compared to end-to-end deep learning approaches.

The study acknowledges that segmentation is challenging due to high intraclass variations in lesion characteristics (color, texture, shape, size, location) and interclass similarity between melanoma and nonmelanoma lesions, which limits the robustness of the segmentation stage.

The authors suggest that the hybrid framework could be simplified by replacing the two pretrained models (VGG-16 and GoogLeNet) with a single enhanced model to reduce computational complexity, indicating a limitation in the current architecture's efficiency.

The classification accuracy on the ISIC 2017 dataset (0.853) was lower than on the ISBI 2016 dataset (0.8892), highlighting potential domain shift or dataset bias issues when applying the model to a different public challenge dataset.
